title: The habit I inherited from my father without meaning to
summary: Describe a small habit you absorbed from a parent that you only noticed years later, and what its presence in you reveals about how character is transmitted.
topic: An unexpected habit inherited from a parent
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a small habit you have inherited from a parent without choosing to, and what that quiet inheritance reveals about how we become like the people who raised us. Use the present perfect, the past perfect, a relative clause, and 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: Three or four years ago, my partner pointed out that I always rinse a teacup twice before pouring tea into it, the way my father has done in our kitchen in Mumbai for as long as I can remember, and I had not…
If anyone had asked me at twenty whether I would grow up to resemble my father in any visible way, I would have insisted I would not, because…
Although I had not deliberately copied this small gesture, what I have come to think it reveals is how character is actually transmitted in a family, which is rarely through…

hintVocab.meaningVi: tiếp thu một cách vô thức
sự giống nhau trong gia đình

hintVocab.phrase: to be set in one's ways
hintVocab.meaningVi: khó thay đổi thói quen của mình
lặp lại một cách vô thức
trở thành ai đó qua thời gian
sự bắt chước không có chủ ý

sampleText:
Three or four years ago, my partner pointed out that I always rinse a teacup twice before pouring tea into it. She was not complaining; she was curious. She said she had watched me do it for two years and had finally asked herself whether I was aware of it. I was not. When I thought about it, however, I realised immediately where it had come from. My father has rinsed every teacup twice in our kitchen in Mumbai for as long as I can remember — once for dust, he says, once for whatever the dust was hiding. I had moved out of that kitchen at nineteen, lived in three other cities since, and had not consciously thought about this small ritual for over a decade. Apparently I had been performing it the whole time.

If anyone had asked me at twenty whether I would grow up to resemble my father in any visible way, I would have insisted I would not. He and I argue about almost everything when we are in the same room, and at twenty I was fairly sure that the project of becoming an adult meant becoming someone other than him. What I had not yet understood was that the visible things you push against in a parent — opinions, attitudes, the way they argue at the dinner table — are not the things you absorb. The things you absorb are smaller and quieter: the order in which they wash their hands, the way they pause before answering the phone, whether they sit down to eat or stand at the counter. These small actions slip into your body before you have a chance to refuse them, and they do not announce themselves on the day they move in.

What I have come to think this small inheritance reveals is how character is actually transmitted in a family, which is rarely through what parents say. Parents over-estimate the importance of their advice and under-estimate the importance of what they do every morning without thinking. Although I would still describe myself as quite different from my father in most ways that matter — politics, work, how I handle disagreement — there is now a layer of him in me that has nothing to do with any of those. It is in how I hold a cup, how I close a door, how I check the lock before leaving the flat. However, I do not find this unsettling any more. It used to feel like a small defeat. Now it feels more like quiet evidence that I belonged to someone before I belonged to myself, and that what was given to me in those years was not just opinions but a set of physical habits I will probably carry for the rest of my life.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect for an unrecognised long-running habit
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'I had moved out of that kitchen at nineteen, lived in three other cities since, and had not consciously thought about this small ritual for over a decade. Apparently I had been performing it the whole time.' kết hợp quá khứ hoàn thành đơn và tiếp diễn để mô tả các sự kiện và thói quen kéo dài trước thời điểm chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'I had moved out of that kitchen at nineteen, lived in three other cities since, and had not consciously thought about this small ritual for over a decade. Apparently I had been performing it the whole time.' combines past perfect simple and continuous to describe events and habits stretching before the main moment.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Third conditional with embedded modal
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If anyone had asked me at twenty whether I would grow up to resemble my father, I would have insisted I would not' kết hợp điều kiện loại 3 với 'would' bên trong mệnh đề 'whether' và một động từ khuyết 'would not' ở cuối — cấu trúc phức tạp nhưng tự nhiên trong văn phản tỉnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If anyone had asked me at twenty whether I would grow up to resemble my father, I would have insisted I would not' combines the third conditional with 'would' inside a 'whether' clause and an elliptical 'would not' at the end — complex but natural in reflective writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Ba bốn năm trước, bạn đời của tôi chỉ ra rằng tôi luôn tráng tách trà hai lần trước khi rót trà vào. Cô ấy không phàn nàn; cô ấy tò mò. Cô ấy nói cô ấy đã quan sát tôi làm điều đó trong hai năm và cuối cùng đã tự hỏi liệu tôi có nhận thức được không. Tôi thì không. Tuy nhiên, khi tôi nghĩ về nó, tôi ngay lập tức nhận ra nó đến từ đâu. Bố tôi đã tráng mọi tách trà hai lần trong căn bếp của chúng tôi ở Mumbai trong khoảng thời gian tôi nhớ được — một lần cho bụi, ông ấy nói, một lần cho bất cứ thứ gì bụi đang che giấu. Tôi đã chuyển ra khỏi căn bếp đó năm mười chín tuổi, sống ở ba thành phố khác kể từ đó, và đã không nghĩ một cách có ý thức về nghi thức nhỏ này trong hơn một thập kỷ. Hóa ra tôi đã thực hiện nó suốt thời gian đó.

Nếu ai đã hỏi tôi ở tuổi hai mươi liệu tôi sẽ lớn lên giống bố theo bất kỳ cách rõ rệt nào, tôi đã sẽ khẳng định là tôi sẽ không. Ông và tôi cãi nhau về gần như mọi thứ khi chúng tôi ở cùng phòng, và ở tuổi hai mươi tôi khá chắc chắn rằng dự án trở thành người lớn có nghĩa là trở thành ai đó khác ông ấy. Điều tôi chưa hiểu là những điều rõ rệt bạn chống lại ở một người cha mẹ — quan điểm, thái độ, cách họ tranh luận trên bàn ăn — không phải là những điều bạn tiếp thu. Những điều bạn tiếp thu nhỏ hơn và lặng lẽ hơn: thứ tự họ rửa tay, cách họ ngừng lại trước khi trả lời điện thoại, họ ngồi xuống để ăn hay đứng ở quầy. Những hành động nhỏ này len vào cơ thể bạn trước khi bạn có cơ hội từ chối chúng, và chúng không tuyên bố sự xuất hiện vào ngày chúng chuyển đến.

Điều tôi đã dần nghĩ về sự thừa kế nhỏ này tiết lộ là cách tính cách thực sự được truyền trong một gia đình, hiếm khi qua những gì cha mẹ nói. Cha mẹ đánh giá quá cao tầm quan trọng của lời khuyên và đánh giá quá thấp tầm quan trọng của những gì họ làm mỗi sáng mà không suy nghĩ. Mặc dù tôi vẫn sẽ mô tả mình khá khác bố tôi theo hầu hết các cách quan trọng — chính trị, công việc, cách tôi xử lý bất đồng — giờ có một lớp ông ấy trong tôi không liên quan gì đến bất kỳ điều nào trong số đó. Nó nằm ở cách tôi cầm một cái cốc, cách tôi đóng một cánh cửa, cách tôi kiểm tra khóa trước khi rời căn hộ. Tuy nhiên, tôi không còn thấy điều này khó chịu nữa. Trước đây nó cảm thấy như một thất bại nhỏ. Giờ nó cảm thấy giống bằng chứng thầm lặng rằng tôi đã thuộc về ai đó trước khi tôi thuộc về chính mình, và những gì được trao cho tôi trong những năm đó không chỉ là quan điểm mà là một bộ thói quen vật lý mà tôi có lẽ sẽ mang theo phần đời còn lại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses past perfect continuous correctly?
options: Apparently I had been performing it the whole time.
Apparently I have been performing it the whole time.
Apparently I had been perform it the whole time.
Apparently I had being performing it the whole time.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect continuous = 'had been + -ing'. The context (past discovery) requires past, not present perfect.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the third conditional correctly?
options: If anyone had asked me at twenty, I would have insisted I would not.
If anyone would have asked me at twenty, I would have insisted I would not.
If anyone had asked me at twenty, I will have insisted I would not.
If anyone had ask me at twenty, I would have insisted I would not.
mcQuestions.explanation: Third conditional: 'had + past participle' in the if-clause; 'would have + past participle' in the main.
mcQuestions.hint: 'If + had + past participle, would have + past participle'.

options: Although I would describe myself as quite different from my father, there is now a layer of him in me.
Although I would describe myself as quite different from my father, but there is now a layer of him in me.
Although, I would describe myself as quite different from my father there is now a layer of him in me.
I would describe myself as quite different from my father although however there is now a layer of him in me.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses present perfect correctly for an ongoing habit?
options: My father has rinsed every teacup twice in our kitchen in Mumbai for as long as I can remember.
My father have rinsed every teacup twice in our kitchen in Mumbai for as long as I can remember.
My father has rinse every teacup twice in our kitchen in Mumbai for as long as I can remember.
My father has been rinse every teacup twice in our kitchen in Mumbai for as long as I can remember.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect with 'for as long as I can remember' = 'has + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause with 'whose' or 'in which' correctly?
options: The order in which they wash their hands.
The order which in they wash their hands.
The order whose they wash their hands.
The order what they wash their hands.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'In which' is the correct relative phrase here; 'whose' is possessive, 'what' is wrong as a relative.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'no longer / not any more'?
options: I do not find this unsettling any more.
I do not find this unsettling no more.
I do not find this unsettling some more.
I do find not this unsettling any more.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not… any more' (or 'any longer') is the standard structure; double negatives ('not… no more') are wrong.

options: These small actions slip into your body before you have a chance to refuse them.
These small actions slip into your body before you having a chance to refuse them.
These small actions slip into your body before you to have a chance to refuse them.
These small actions slip into your body before that you have a chance to refuse them.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Before + subject + verb' is the standard subordinate-clause structure; no 'to', no '-ing', no 'that'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'have nothing to do with + noun' correctly?
options: A layer of him in me that has nothing to do with any of those.
A layer of him in me that has nothing to do for any of those.
A layer of him in me that has nothing doing with any of those.
A layer of him in me that have nothing to do with any of those.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have nothing to do with + noun' is the fixed idiom meaning 'not be connected to'. Singular 'layer' takes 'has'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'how + clause' as a noun phrase correctly?
options: It is in how I hold a cup, how I close a door, how I check the lock before leaving the flat.
It is in how do I hold a cup, how do I close a door, how do I check the lock before leaving the flat.
It is in how to I hold a cup, how to I close a door, how to I check the lock before leaving the flat.
It is in the how I hold a cup, the how I close a door, the how I check the lock before leaving the flat.
mcQuestions.explanation: Embedded 'how + subject + verb' uses statement word order, no 'do/does', no 'to', no article.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'used to + base verb' for a past state?
options: It used to feel like a small defeat.
It use to feel like a small defeat.
It used to feeling like a small defeat.
It was used to feel like a small defeat.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used to + base verb' for a past state that no longer applies. 'Use to' (without -d) is wrong; 'was used to' has a different meaning.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Used to + base verb' for past state.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'will probably + base verb' correctly?
options: A set of physical habits I will probably carry for the rest of my life.
A set of physical habits I will probably carrying for the rest of my life.
A set of physical habits I probably will carry for the rest of my life.
A set of physical habits I will probably to carry for the rest of my life.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Will + probably + base verb' is standard. 'Probably' typically sits between the auxiliary and the main verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If we inherit our parents' physical habits without choosing to, are those habits really 'ours'? And if a parent's everyday actions shape us more than their words, does that change how you would want to behave around children — your own or anyone's?
